blocks.text: An option is a contract on 100 shares of an underlying stock. The BUYER (holder, long) pays a premium for a RIGHT. The SELLER (writer, short) collects the premium and takes on an OBLIGATION if the buyer exercises. Every option question starts with sorting out those two roles. A derivative's value 'derives' from the underlying — the option itself raises no capital for the issuer and gives the holder no dividends or votes.

blocks.term: In / at / out of the money
blocks.definition: Calls are in the money when market price is ABOVE the strike; puts when market is BELOW the strike. 'In the money' ignores the premium — it's purely strike vs market. Intrinsic value = the in-the-money amount (never negative); the rest of the premium is time value, which decays to zero at expiration.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: With XYZ at $53, an XYZ 50 call is in the money by $3 (intrinsic value $3). If its premium is $4.50, time value is $1.50. An XYZ 55 put is in the money by $2. The 50 call buyer breaks even at $54.50 — strike plus premium. At expiration an option is worth exactly its intrinsic value; out-of-the-money options expire worthless.

Breakevens: for calls, strike + premium (both buyer and writer — same number, opposite hopes). For puts, strike − premium. Exercise runs through the Options Clearing Corporation (OCC), which issues, guarantees, and standardizes all listed options — the writer who gets assigned is chosen from the pool (random or FIFO at the firm, never 'largest position first'). Equity options are American-style (exercisable any time); most index options are European-style (exercise only at expiration), and index options settle in CASH, not shares.

Standard terms: one contract = 100 shares; equity options expire on the third Friday of the expiration month; standard expirations run up to 9 months (longer-dated LEAPS run up to 39 months). A premium of 4.50 means $450 per contract. Contracts adjust for splits and stock dividends (a 2-for-1 split turns one 60-strike contract into two 30-strike contracts) but NOT for ordinary cash dividends. Buyers of options must receive the Options Disclosure Document (ODD) at or before account approval, and a designated options principal must approve the account before the first trade.

Call UP, put DOWN: calls go in the money when the stock rises above the strike, puts when it falls below. Only the uncovered (naked) call writer faces unlimited risk — the exam's favorite 'riskiest position.' And exercise gives the holder stock in two ways: exercising a call means BUYING at the strike; being assigned on a put means BUYING at the strike.

The SIE keeps strategies simple: single positions plus the two covered/income plays. Buy calls to profit from a rise with limited, leveraged risk — control of 100 shares for a few hundred dollars. Buy puts to profit from a fall — or to protect a stock you own. Write options to generate income when you expect the stock to sit still. Time works FOR the writer and AGAINST the buyer: every day that passes melts time value.

blocks.definition: Own 100 shares AND write a call against them. The premium adds income and cushions small declines; the trade-off is capping upside at the strike. The most conservative option-writing strategy — the obligation to deliver is already covered by the shares.

blocks.title: Example — covered call
blocks.text: Own 100 XYZ at $48; write an XYZ 50 call for a $2 premium. Stock at $55 at expiration: shares are called away at $50 — you net $50 − $48 + $2 = $4/share and miss the rest of the rally. Stock at $47: the call expires, you keep the $2, effective cost basis now $46. Breakeven: $46 (cost − premium). Max gain: $4/share; max loss: $46/share if the stock goes to zero.

The naked (uncovered) call writer has no shares to deliver. If the stock doubles, they must buy at market to sell at the strike — theoretically unlimited loss, which is why naked call writing requires the highest account approval level. A naked put writer's worst case is the stock going to zero: they must buy worthless stock at the strike, losing strike − premium. A cash-secured put writer sets aside the strike price in cash — a way to get paid while waiting to buy a stock cheaper.

blocks.title: Example — long put
blocks.text: Buy an ABC 60 put for $3 with ABC at $61. ABC falls to $50: exercise to sell at $60 what you can buy at $50 — $10 intrinsic minus $3 premium = $7/share profit ($700). Maximum gain if ABC hits zero: $60 − $3 = $5,700. Maximum loss either way: the $300 premium. Compare a short seller: same bearish view, but unlimited risk and margin required.

blocks.caption: Pick the strategy
rows: Strongly bullish, limited cash, max leverage
Neutral-to-bullish, wants income, would buy the stock lower
Expects volatility but unsure of direction
Buy a straddle (call + put, same strike)

blocks.text: One step beyond the basics that occasionally appears: a long straddle (buy a call AND a put, same strike/expiration) profits from a BIG move in either direction — the risk is both premiums if the stock sits still. A short straddle collects both premiums betting on no movement, with unlimited risk on the call side.

Buyers of options: maximum loss = premium, always. Writers: maximum gain = premium, always. If the question asks 'most you can lose' for any long option, the answer is what you paid. Most options expire unexercised — buyers usually close by selling, and positions can always be closed with an offsetting trade instead of exercised.

Hedging means buying insurance for an existing position. The rule of thumb: to hedge, BUY the option that profits when your position loses. Long stock is hurt by a fall — buy a put. Short stock is hurt by a rise — buy a call. Writing an option against a position only earns premium income; it never fully protects.

blocks.definition: Long stock + long put. The put guarantees a selling price (the strike) no matter how far the stock falls — a price floor, for the cost of the premium. Maximum loss: (stock cost − strike) + premium. Sometimes called 'married put' when bought with the stock.

blocks.title: Example — protective put
blocks.text: Own 100 DEF at $80; buy a DEF 75 put for $2. Worst case: DEF collapses to $40, you exercise and sell at $75. Loss = ($80 − $75) + $2 = $7/share instead of $40. If DEF rallies to $95, the put expires and your gain is $15 − $2 = $13/share — insurance you didn't need but were glad to have. Breakeven on the combined position: $82.

A short seller borrowed and sold stock, hoping to repurchase lower. Their nightmare is a rally — losses are unlimited. Buying a call caps the repurchase price at the strike: maximum loss becomes (strike − sale price) + premium. Writing options against a position ('covered' writing) only cushions by the premium — it does NOT establish a floor or ceiling. Full protection requires BUYING the option.

blocks.title: Example — hedging a short
blocks.text: Short 100 GHI at $60; buy a GHI 65 call for $3. GHI spikes to $90: exercise the call, buy at $65, cover the short. Loss = ($65 − $60) + $3 = $8/share — instead of $33 and still climbing.

Beyond single stocks, the same logic scales up: a portfolio manager fearing a market drop can buy broad index puts (cash-settled) instead of selling every holding; an exporter expecting foreign-currency payments can hedge with currency options; a bond manager can hedge rate risk with yield-based or Treasury options. The instrument changes; the rule doesn't — buy the option that pays when your position hurts.

'Best protection' = BUY an option (put protects long stock; call protects short stock). 'Generate income' = WRITE an option. If the question says both protection and income, protection wins the 'best hedge' phrasing every time. A collar (own stock + buy put + write call) does both: floor from the put, the call premium pays for it, upside capped.
